title: Chapter 4 Quiz
description: Test your knowledge of Chapter 4 concepts!

questions.question: Which keyword is used to conditionally execute code only when a condition is true?
questions.explanation: The 'if' statement executes its block only if the provided condition evaluates to true.

questions.question: An 'else' statement does NOT have its own condition inside parentheses ().
questions.explanation: An 'else' block acts as a catch-all and executes automatically when the preceding 'if' is false.

questions.question: Which of these allows you to check for multiple, sequential alternative conditions?
questions.explanation: You chain additional conditions to an 'if' sequence using 'else if'.

questions.question: The ternary operator shorthand for if-else uses the _____ and : symbols.
questions.explanation: The syntax is (condition) ? true_expr : false_expr;

questions.question: When does the C++ compiler stop executing an if / else if / else chain?
options: It checks and runs every block.
It stops at the first true condition, executes it, and ignores the rest.
It runs the very last true condition.
questions.explanation: The compiler checks sequentially from top to bottom and breaks the chain completely after finding the first true condition.

questions.question: You can use an 'else' block by itself, without an 'if' block before it.
questions.explanation: An 'else' block MUST immediately follow an 'if' or 'else if' block.

questions.question: In a switch statement, the _____ keyword is used to exit the switch block.
questions.explanation: The 'break' keyword stops the compiler from running more code inside the switch.

questions.question: In a switch block, what keyword acts like the final 'else' statement?
questions.explanation: The 'default' case handles situations where no other case matches the expression.

questions.question: What happens if you omit the 'break' statement within a switch case?
options: It falls through and executes the code for subsequent cases as well.
It loops back to the start of the switch statement.
questions.explanation: Without a 'break', C++ performs 'fall-through' and runs code in the following cases until it hits a break or the end of the block.

questions.question: The condition inside an 'if' statement must evaluate to a boolean (true or false).
questions.explanation: The if statement relies on a condition resolving to either 1 (true) or 0 (false).

questions.question: What is the correct syntax for a simple 'if' statement in C++?
questions.explanation: Notice the lowercase 'if', and parentheses () around the condition.

questions.question: To start a switch statement evaluating variable 'x', you type switch ( _____ ).
questions.explanation: switch (x) { ... }
Since 15 is not >= 18, it returns the false expression: 'Child'.

questions.question: Switch statements are often cleaner than chaining 5 or more else-if conditions testing the same variable for equality.
questions.explanation: When comparing a single variable against many specific values, a switch statement is more readable and slightly more performant.
The second block runs because y == 5 is the first true condition. The compiler then skips the rest (including z == 15).

questions.question: The symbol separating the true value and false value in the ternary operator is a _____.
questions.explanation: A colon (:) implies 'otherwise' in the ternary short-hand.

questions.question: What happens if multiple cases have the same matching value in a switch statement?
questions.explanation: C++ does not allow duplicate case values within the same switch block.

questions.question: A switch statement condition can evaluate a string data type in C++ out of the box.
questions.explanation: In standard C++, a switch statement condition must evaluate to an integer or an enumerated (enum) value, or char.

questions.question: Which of these is NOT a conditional statement?
questions.explanation: 'when' is not a keyword or conditional mechanism in C++.

questions.question: An 'if' inside another 'if' is known as a _____ if statement.
questions.explanation: Placing an 'if' block completely inside another 'if' block is called nesting.
